summary: A commercial should organize every beat around one primary promise, not a scattered list of product advantages.
principles: Choose the one promise the audience should remember after the ad ends — this is the ad's reason to exist.
Treat every secondary feature as proof for the primary promise, not a separate message competing for attention.
The CTA must repeat or complete the same promise — a CTA that introduces a new benefit undoes the positioning work.
The single promise should be the intersection of what the product genuinely delivers and what the audience most wants to believe.
Every scene that doesn't serve the single promise should be cut or reframed — luxury of focus is the primary creative discipline.
The promise should be specific enough to be testable: 'takes better photos' is vague, 'captures the color your eyes see' is specific and memorable.
If the brief lists competing promises, the positioning decision must be made in the bible stage and locked — downstream stages must not reopen it.
apply_when: The brief lists many selling points.
The product is complex, premium, technical, or feature-rich.
The campaign needs a clear brand memory structure.
avoid_when: The user explicitly asks for a catalogue, spec sheet, or comparison table.
Regulated disclosure requires multiple independent claims.
The ad is a retargeting variant for already-qualified buyers.
failure_patterns: Three equal promises in one ad: the viewer remembers none of them and the ad feels like a product listing.
Promise that's a category descriptor, not a brand differentiator: 'fast performance' when every competitor claims the same.
CTA that pivots to a different promise than the one the ad just made — the viewer's trust is broken.
Promise so generic it could apply to any product in the category — no positioning has actually occurred.

cross_domain_notes.note: The single promise must be the one the audience most wants to believe, not necessarily the strongest technical claim the product team prefers.
The arc type is determined by the promise: promise of resolution → problem-solution, promise of transformation → journey, promise of proof → social-proof.
The hook must activate the single promise in the first second — if the viewer can't identify the promise from the hook, positioning failed.

execution_techniques: Write the single promise as a single sentence on paper before writing anything else. If it takes two sentences, it's two promises.
Test each scene against the promise: if you can remove the scene without weakening the promise's credibility, the scene is unnecessary.
End the CTA with the promise restated in action language: 'See the difference' not 'Learn more about our technology.'
When the brief lists five selling points, rank them and commit to one as the star — the other four become one-line proof points.
